firstAid.subtitle: CARE & CALM
firstAid.title: Behavioral SOS Guide
firstAid.desc: Managing behavioral changes like agitation, wandering, and sundowning requires patience and specific techniques. Follow these evidence-based strategies.
firstAid.step1Desc: Your loved one can sense your frustration. Keep your voice low and your movements slow to avoid escalating the situation.
firstAid.step2Desc: Agitation is often a form of communication. Check if they are hungry, thirsty, in pain, or need the bathroom.
firstAid.step3Desc: Use simple words and phrases. Hold their hands or provide a gentle touch if they find it comforting.
doList: Redirect attention to a familiar, calming activity.
Keep the environment quiet and well-lit to reduce confusion.
dontList: Don't argue or try to use logic to explain reality.
Don't use physical restraint unless there is immediate danger.
Don't raise your voice or show obvious frustration.
Don't ask complex questions with multiple choices.
firstAid.emergencyTitle: When to Call for Professional Help
emergencyList: When the person becomes physically aggressive and a danger to themselves or others.
Sudden, unexplained changes in health or behavior (may indicate an infection).
If the person wanders away and is not found immediately.
Severe agitation that does not respond to calming techniques.
Refusing essential medications for more than 24 hours.

aboutAlzheimer.subtitle: Understand
aboutAlzheimer.title: Alzheimer's Pathophysiology
aboutAlzheimer.desc: Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by cognitive decline and specific pathological hallmarks.
aboutAlzheimer.classSubtitle: Select a stage or hallmark to view detailed clinical patterns and care notes.

what.title: What is Alzheimer's?
what.content: Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia — a continuous decline in thinking, behavioral and social skills that affects a person's ability to function independently. It involves the progressive death of brain cells.

causes.title: Biological Causes
causes.content: The exact causes aren't fully understood, but at its core are brain proteins that fail to function normally. This disrupts the work of brain cells (neurons) and triggers a series of toxic events.

diagnosis.title: The Pathology (Hallmarks)
diagnosis.content:
The main biological changes include:
1. Amyloid-β Plaques: Protein clumps that block cell signaling.
2. Tau Tangles: Twisted fibers of another protein that destroy cell transport.
3. Neuroinflammation: Chronic immune response damaging brain tissue.
4. Cholinergic Deficit: Loss of key memory neurotransmitters.

amyloid.name: Amyloid-β Plaques
amyloid.title: Amyloid Beta
amyloid.description: Extracellular deposition of amyloid-beta protein fragments that clump together, disrupting nerve-to-nerve communication and triggering immune responses.
amyloid.note: Modern drugs like Aduhelm target these plaques directly to slow disease progression.

tau.name: Tau Tangles
tau.title: Tau Proteins
tau.description: Intracellular formation of neurofibrillary tangles. Normally tau proteins stabilize microtubules, but in AD, they collapse into twisted strands.
tau.note: Tau accumulation correlates more closely with cognitive decline than amyloid plaques.

cholinergic.name: Cholinergic Deficit
cholinergic.title: Acetylcholine Loss
cholinergic.description: A significant decrease in acetylcholine synthesis and activity. This neurotransmitter is crucial for learning and memory processes. The goal of treatment is to overcome this presynaptic deficit.
cholinergic.note: Donepezil is a selective AChEI with a long half-life (approx. 100h in elderly), allowing once-daily dosing.

inflammation.name: Neuroinflammation
inflammation.title: Immune Response
inflammation.description: Chronic activation of microglia and astrocytes. While meant to protect, overactive immune cells release neurotoxins and inflammatory agents.
inflammation.note: Managing cardiovascular health helps reduce systemic inflammation that affects the brain.

mci.name: Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI)
mci.title: MCI Stage
mci.description: The stage between the expected cognitive decline of normal aging and the more serious decline of dementia.
mci.note: Early detection at this stage is crucial for starting pharmacological interventions.

mild.name: Mild Alzheimer's (Early Stage)
mild.title: Mild Stage
mild.description: A person may function independently but experiences memory lapses that affect daily life.
mild.note: Focus on maintaining routines and using memory aids like the Sanad Behavior Log.

moderate.name: Moderate Alzheimer's (Middle Stage)
moderate.title: Moderate Stage
moderate.description: The longest stage, where patients require more help with daily activities. NMDA antagonists like Memantine are often introduced here.
moderate.note: Memantine acts as an NMDA antagonist, protecting neurons against glutamate-induced excitotoxicity.

severe.name: Severe AD (Late-stage)
severe.title: Severe Stage
severe.description: Individuals lose the ability to respond to their environment, carry on a conversation, and eventually control movement.
severe.note: Focus shifts to palliative care and maintaining comfort and dignity.

mythsFacts.subtitle: CLARITY
mythsFacts.title: Myths vs. Facts
mythsFacts.desc: Alzheimer's is often misunderstood. Let's clear up some common misconceptions.

items.myth: Alzheimer's is a normal part of aging.
items.fact: It is a progressive brain disease. While age is a risk factor, AD involves significant cell death and is not an inevitable part of growing older.

items.myth: Memory loss is the only symptom of Alzheimer's.
items.fact: AD also affects mood, behavior, language, and physical coordination. Personality changes are often more distressing than memory loss.

specialWarnings.subtitle: Caregiver Vigilance
specialWarnings.title: Population-Specific Risks
specialWarnings.desc: Alzheimer's treatments require careful monitoring, especially in seniors who often have multiple health conditions.

items.title: Psychosocial Impact
items.content: People diagnosed in their 40s or 50s may still be working or raising families. The impact on career and family dynamics is profound and requires specialized support.

items.title: Genetic Factors
items.content: Early-onset cases are more likely to have a strong genetic component (PSEN1, PSEN2, APP mutations). Genetic counseling is often recommended for the family.

items.title: Polypharmacy Risks
items.content: Most AD patients are over 65 and take multiple medications. The risk of drug-drug interactions with AD medications is significantly high.

items.title: GI Sensitivity
items.content: Cholinesterase inhibitors (Donepezil, Rivastigmine) frequently cause nausea and vomiting, which can lead to weight loss and dehydration in the elderly.

items.title: Cardiovascular Effects
items.content: These drugs can slow the heart rate (bradycardia), which may lead to fainting and dangerous falls in older adults.

checker.subtitle: Safe Prescribing
checker.title: AD Medication Safety
checker.desc: Check your prescriptions against core Alzheimer's therapies and identify dangerous food or drug interactions.
checker.buildDesc: Add medications below. We will cross-reference them with standard AD treatments.
checker.awaitingDesc: Enter the patient's medications to receive a comprehensive safety report based on clinical protocols.
checker.noInteractions: No major interactions found in our clinical database for these inputs. Always confirm with the patient's doctor.
checker.noFoodWarnings: No specific food restrictions identified for these medications in our database.
checker.disclaimerDesc: This tool is for educational support for caregivers. It is not a replacement for professional medical advice. Never change a patient's medication without a doctor's order.
checker.archiveDesc: Sign in to save patient medication history.

dashboard.secureDesc: Access the patient's behavior logs and medication schedule.

auth.loginDesc: Secure access to your care tools
auth.signupDesc: Empowering you to provide better care

about.title: About Aoun (عون)
about.desc: Aoun is a dedicated platform designed to support Alzheimer's caregivers with evidence-based insights and management tools.
about.missionText1: Caregiving for someone with Alzheimer's is a heroic yet challenging task. We observed that caregivers often lack immediate tools to manage behavioral crises and track complex medication regimens.
about.missionText2: Aoun provides real-time drug interaction checks, behavioral management guides, and a digital log to ensure that both the patient and the caregiver are supported.

values.title: Evidence-Based
values.desc: Our guides follow clinical research from the Alzheimer's Association and pharmacological databases.

values.title: Compassionate
values.desc: The UI is designed to be calm and intuitive, reducing the stress of daily care management.

values.title: Safety First
values.desc: Preventing medication errors and behavioral escalations is our primary goal.

values.title: Empowering
values.desc: We give you the knowledge to handle difficult situations with confidence.

about.ctaText: Learn how to manage sundowning and agitation using our clinical behavioral management tool.

home.heroDescription: Your digital partner in Alzheimer's care. Smart tracking, medication safety, and expert behavioral guides.
home.understandingDescription: Alzheimer's is a complex neurodegenerative disease involving amyloid-β plaques and tau tangles. Understanding its pathophysiology is vital for precise care.
home.servicesSubtitle: Tools designed for safety and peace of mind.
home.eduDesc: Comprehensive guides on handling agitation, wandering, and sundowning to ensure patient safety.
home.checkerDesc: Safety tool for cross-verifying Alzheimer's prescriptions against daily medications.
home.archivesDesc: Securely track behavior, appetite, and medication adherence in your caregiver dashboard.
home.ctaDesc: Join Aoun today to build a safer, more organized care routine for your loved one.
